---
title: "Rung 1 · Causal discovery"
---

[Setup.]{.lead-in} Observational data and a willingness to treat any learned
graph as a *hypothesis*.

[Move.]{.lead-in} Run one or more discovery algorithms (PC, GES, LiNGAM,
NOTEARS) and compare the recovered structure to what domain knowledge allows.

## Discovery is a tool, not an oracle

Algorithms recover a [CPDAG](../glossary.qmd) — an equivalence class — not the
truth. Crucially, **they disagree with each other**. On the toy DAG:

| Algorithm | Skeleton F1 vs truth | Note |
|---|---|---|
| PC | 0.50 | constraint-based; misses edges it cannot orient |
| GES | 1.00 | score-based; recovers the skeleton here |
| DirectLiNGAM | 0.80 | exploits non-Gaussianity |

That disagreement is not a nuisance — it is *signal*. It feeds directly into the
[complexity score](02-complexity-score.qmd).

## Constraints make discovery honest

Pure data-driven discovery produces impossible edges (e.g. `Outcome → Age`). The
fix is to encode domain knowledge as **forbidden** and **required** edges and
**temporal tiers**, so expert priors become first-class citizens. The
[interactive app](../app.qmd) lets you toggle these live and watch the recovered
graph change.

::: {.pullquote}
"The causes are not in the data." — Judea Pearl. And: "You cannot discover what
you did not measure." No algorithm recovers a confounder you never recorded.
:::

[Caveat.]{.lead-in} Discovery cannot detect unmeasured confounding, cannot prove
causation, and cannot replace domain expertise. Use it to *generate* structure to
critique, then move on.

Next: [score how much this all matters →](02-complexity-score.qmd)
